controlled-use-of-fire.summary: Fire evidence from Wonderwerk Cave, South Africa dates to ~1 million years ago. Deliberate control enabled cooking, warmth, and protection — reshaping human biology and culture.
key_facts: Key sites: Wonderwerk Cave, South Africa

agriculture-and-domestication.key_figures: Multiple independent centres: Fertile Crescent, China, Mesoamerica, Africa
agriculture-and-domestication.summary: Agriculture emerged independently in at least seven regions worldwide, enabling permanent settlement, population growth, and the rise of the first cities.
key_facts: First crops: wheat, barley, millet, rice
First animals: goats, sheep, cattle, pigs

the-wheel.summary: The wheel appeared in Mesopotamia ~3500 BCE. The key insight was the axle, allowing free rotation. The wheel's principle underpins gears, turbines, engines, and all machinery.
key_facts: Americas and Africa developed without it

writing-systems.key_figures: Sumer (cuneiform); Egypt (hieroglyphs); China and Mesoamerica (independent inventions)
writing-systems.summary: Writing makes it possible to transmit knowledge across time and space with precision.
key_facts: Alphabet invented in ancient Levant ~1800 BCE

mathematics-and-the-concept-of-zero.key_figures: Babylonian, Egyptian, Indian (Brahmagupta), and Maya mathematicians
mathematics-and-the-concept-of-zero.summary: Mathematics is the language of science, engineering, finance, and computing.
key_facts: Hindu-Arabic numerals spread via Islamic world

paper.key_figures: Cai Lun (Han Dynasty China); later Islamic and European paper mills
paper.summary: Paper made written records cheaper, lighter, and more portable than parchment or papyrus.
key_facts: Invented in Han Dynasty China, 105 CE
Reached Europe via Islamic Spain: 12th century
Made books affordable before printing press

the-compass.key_figures: Han Dynasty China; independently developed in Europe c. 12th century
the-compass.summary: The compass allowed sailors to navigate with confidence beyond sight of coastlines and stars.

gunpowder.key_figures: Chinese alchemists (Tang Dynasty); spread to Islamic world and Europe by 13th century
gunpowder.summary: Gunpowder fundamentally altered warfare, siege technology, mining, quarrying, and later civil engineering.
key_facts: Discovered by Tang Dynasty alchemists ~850 CE
Reached Europe via Islamic scholars: 13th century
Later enabled mining and civil engineering

the-printing-press.key_figures: Johannes Gutenberg (Europe); Bi Sheng (China, c. 1040 CE, earlier movable type)
the-printing-press.summary: Before the printing press, each book had to be copied by hand — a process taking months, affordable only to the wealthy or the Church.
key_facts: Movable type in China: Bi Sheng, ~1040 CE

the-scientific-method.key_figures: Ibn al-Haytham (optics, c. 1021); Francis Bacon; Galileo Galilei; René Descartes
the-scientific-method.summary: The scientific method is the most powerful framework yet devised for building reliable knowledge about the natural world.
key_facts: Ibn al-Haytham's 'Book of Optics': c. 1021 CE
Foundation of all modern science and tech

the-telescope.key_figures: Hans Lipperhey (inventor, Netherlands); Galileo Galilei (first systematic astronomical use)
the-telescope.summary: The telescope transformed astronomy from a mathematical and philosophical discipline into an observational science.
key_facts: Revealed moons of Jupiter, craters on Moon

the-microscope.key_figures: Zacharias Janssen (compound microscope, c. 1590); Antoni van Leeuwenhoek (high-power single-lens)
the-microscope.summary: The microscope revealed that life at scales invisible to the naked eye was extraordinarily complex and abundant.
key_facts: First observations of bacteria and protozoa
Electron microscopes now image individual atoms

newtonian-mechanics.summary: Newtonian mechanics provided a single coherent framework for understanding all motion — from falling apples to planetary orbits, from pendulums to projectiles.
key_facts: Three laws of motion + law of gravitation

steam-engine.key_figures: Thomas Newcomen; James Watt (improved design); Richard Trevithick (high-pressure steam)
steam-engine.summary: The steam engine was the prime mover of the Industrial Revolution.

electricity-generation-and-distribution.key_figures: Michael Faraday; Thomas Edison; Nikola Tesla; George Westinghouse
electricity-generation-and-distribution.summary: Electrical power distribution is the infrastructure that underlies virtually all of modern life.
key_facts: Edison's DC system vs Tesla's AC: 1880s–1890s
Most versatile energy form ever developed

electromagnetism.key_figures: Hans Christian Ørsted; André-Marie Ampère; Michael Faraday; James Clerk Maxwell
electromagnetism.summary: Maxwell's equations are one of the greatest intellectual achievements in physics.
key_facts: Predicted light, radio, X-rays as EM waves
Foundation of all motors, radios, Wi-Fi

internal-combustion-engine.key_figures: Étienne Lenoir; Nikolaus Otto (four-stroke cycle, 1876); Rudolf Diesel (1897)
internal-combustion-engine.summary: The internal combustion engine (ICE) powered the automobile, the aeroplane, the motorcycle, the tractor, and the ship in the 20th century.
key_facts: Compact design enabled cars and aircraft
Now driving transition to electric vehicles

automobile.key_figures: Karl Benz; Gottlieb Daimler; Henry Ford (mass production)
automobile.summary: The automobile created an entirely new industrial ecosystem: steel, rubber, glass, petroleum refining, road construction, urban planning, insurance, tourism, and logistics.
key_facts: Ford Model T price fell from $825 to $260

airplane.key_figures: Orville and Wilbur Wright; later: Louis Blériot, Anthony Fokker, Frank Whittle (jet engine)
airplane.summary: Aviation compressed the globe, making intercontinental travel routine rather than extraordinary.
key_facts: First powered, heavier-than-air flight at Kitty Hawk

refrigeration.key_figures: Jacob Perkins; Carl von Linde; Clarence Birdseye (commercial frozen food)
refrigeration.summary: Refrigeration is one of the most consequential public health technologies ever invented.
key_facts: Enables vaccine and medicine cold chains

vaccination.key_figures: Edward Jenner (smallpox); Louis Pasteur (rabies, cholera); Jonas Salk (polio)
vaccination.summary: Vaccination is the most cost-effective public health intervention ever developed.
key_facts: mRNA vaccines developed in 2020 for COVID-19

antibiotics.key_figures: Alexander Fleming (discovery, 1928); Howard Florey and Ernst Chain (development, 1940s)
antibiotics.summary: Antibiotics made bacterial infections — previously often fatal — reliably treatable.

germ-theory-of-disease.key_figures: Louis Pasteur; Robert Koch; Ignaz Semmelweis; John Snow
germ-theory-of-disease.summary: Germ theory is the conceptual foundation of modern infectious disease medicine, surgery, food safety, and public health.
key_facts: Snow traced cholera to water pump: 1854
Koch identified TB and cholera bacteria

anaesthesia.key_figures: Crawford Long (ether, 1842); William Morton (1846); James Young Simpson (chloroform, 1847)
anaesthesia.summary: Morton's public demonstration of ether anaesthesia at Massachusetts General Hospital on 16 October 1846 is the conventional start date. Chloroform anaesthesia, introduced by Simpson in 1847, transformed surgery worldwide.
key_facts: First ether use in surgery: Long, 1842
Modern anaesthesia uses sophisticated drug combos

x-rays-and-medical-imaging.key_figures: Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen (X-rays, 1895); Godfrey Hounsfield and Allan Cormack (CT, 1971)
x-rays-and-medical-imaging.summary: Medical imaging allows physicians to see inside the human body without surgery.
key_facts: Now includes PET, ultrasound, functional MRI

sanitation-and-clean-water-systems.key_figures: Joseph Bazalgette (London sewers); Edwin Chadwick; John Snow; Lemuel Shattuck
sanitation-and-clean-water-systems.summary: Clean water and sanitation are arguably the single most important public health infrastructure ever built.

dna-structure-and-molecular-genetics.key_figures: James Watson, Francis Crick; Rosalind Franklin (X-ray crystallography); Erwin Chargaff; Oswald Avery
dna-structure-and-molecular-genetics.summary: The double helix is the molecular key to heredity, evolution, and life itself.
key_facts: Avery showed DNA carries genetic info: 1944
Watson and Crick double helix: April 1953

human-genome-sequencing.key_figures: Human Genome Project consortium (NIH, Wellcome Trust, multiple nations); Craig Venter (Celera Genomics)
human-genome-sequencing.summary: The human genome sequence is a complete parts list of human biology.
key_facts: Cost fell from $3B in 2003 to under $200 by 2024

crispr-gene-editing.key_figures: Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier (Nobel Prize 2020); Feng Zhang (independent parallel work)
crispr-gene-editing.summary: CRISPR allows scientists to cut DNA at any specified location and insert, delete, or modify genetic sequences.

the-periodic-table.key_figures: Dmitri Mendeleev; Lothar Meyer (independent parallel work)
the-periodic-table.summary: The periodic table is the foundational organising framework of chemistry.
key_facts: All three predicted elements confirmed by 1886
Modern table organised by atomic number (1913)

quantum-mechanics.key_figures: Max Planck; Niels Bohr; Werner Heisenberg; Erwin Schrödinger; Paul Dirac; Albert Einstein
quantum-mechanics.summary: Quantum mechanics is the most precisely tested scientific theory in history — its predictions agree with experiment to one part in ten billion in some cases.

theory-of-relativity.summary: General relativity is the foundation of modern cosmology.
key_facts: GPS requires relativistic corrections to work

nuclear-energy-and-nuclear-science.key_figures: Otto Hahn, Lise Meitner, Fritz Strassmann (fission); Enrico Fermi (reactor); Manhattan Project team
nuclear-energy-and-nuclear-science.summary: Nuclear science revealed a new dimension of physical reality — that the nucleus of atoms contains enormous energy, accessible through fission and fusion.

plastics-and-synthetic-materials.key_figures: Leo Baekeland (Bakelite); Wallace Carothers (nylon); DuPont chemists
plastics-and-synthetic-materials.summary: Plastics are lightweight, cheap, durable, chemically resistant, and formable into almost any shape.
key_facts: Nylon developed by Carothers at DuPont: 1935

fertilisers-and-modern-agricultural-chem.key_figures: Fritz Haber; Carl Bosch (BASF); Norman Borlaug (Green Revolution)
fertilisers-and-modern-agricultural-chem.summary: The Haber-Bosch process has been described as the most important chemical reaction in history.
key_facts: ~50% of human body nitrogen from this process
Green Revolution saved ~1B people from famine
Nitrogen runoff causes major water pollution

telegraph.key_figures: Samuel Morse; Alfred Vail; William Cooke; Charles Wheatstone
telegraph.summary: The telegraph was the first technology to separate communication from physical transport.
key_facts: Created Reuters, AP, and financial wire services

telephone.key_figures: Alexander Graham Bell; Elisha Gray (disputed co-inventor); Antonio Meucci
telephone.summary: The telephone transformed personal, commercial, and governmental communication by enabling real-time two-way voice communication over distance.
key_facts: Legal disputes with Gray and Meucci
Became AT&T, dominated US for a century
Phone network became early internet backbone

radio.key_figures: Guglielmo Marconi; Nikola Tesla; Heinrich Hertz (discovery of radio waves, 1887)
radio.summary: Radio was the first wireless communication technology, enabling communication without physical cables.
key_facts: Radio was decisive in both World Wars
Underpins Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GPS, mobile phones

transistor.key_figures: William Shockley, John Bardeen, Walter Brattain (Bell Labs)
transistor.summary: The transistor is the fundamental building block of all modern electronics.

integrated-circuit-microchip.key_figures: Jack Kilby (Texas Instruments); Robert Noyce (Fairchild Semiconductor)
integrated-circuit-microchip.summary: The integrated circuit enabled the miniaturisation and cost reduction of electronics that has driven the digital age.
key_facts: Intel founded by Noyce and Moore: 1968

computer.key_figures: Alan Turing (theoretical foundation); Konrad Zuse (Z3, 1941); ENIAC team (1945); John von Neumann
computer.summary: The computer is a universal machine: it can simulate any other machine given sufficient time and memory.
key_facts: Universal machine: same hardware, any software

internet.key_figures: ARPA/DARPA team; Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn (TCP/IP, 1974); Tim Berners-Lee (WWW, 1989–1991)
internet.summary: The internet is the most significant communication infrastructure in human history.
key_facts: Carries ~5 exabytes of data per day

world-wide-web.key_figures: Tim Berners-Lee; Robert Cailliau; Marc Andreessen (Mosaic browser)
world-wide-web.summary: The web is the user-facing layer of the internet — a system of linked documents (HTML) accessible through browsers.
key_facts: No patents: made available to all freely

search-engines.key_figures: Alan Emtage (Archie, 1990); Larry Page and Sergey Brin (Google, 1998)
search-engines.summary: Search engines transformed access to knowledge from a librarian-mediated or encyclopaedia-lookup process to an instant, universal, self-directed one.
key_facts: AI chatbots now beginning to challenge search

satellite-technology.key_figures: Soviet space programme (Sputnik); NASA; Arthur C. Clarke (geostationary orbit concept, 1945)
satellite-technology.summary: Satellites enabled global telecommunications, live international television, weather forecasting, GPS navigation, Earth observation, scientific research, and military surveillance.

gps-global-positioning-system.key_figures: US Department of Defense; Roger L. Easton, Ivan Getting, Bradford Parkinson
gps-global-positioning-system.summary: GPS determines position and time anywhere on Earth with metre-level accuracy using signals from a constellation of satellites in medium Earth orbit.

semiconductor-technology.key_figures: Bell Labs; Texas Instruments; Intel; TSMC; ASML
semiconductor-technology.summary: Semiconductors are the physical substrate of all digital technology.

laser.key_figures: Theodore Maiman (first ruby laser, 1960); Charles Townes and Arthur Schawlow (theoretical basis, MASER)
laser.summary: Lasers produce coherent, monochromatic, highly directional light.
key_facts: LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission
Used in LASIK, fibre optics, manufacturing
LiDAR in self-driving vehicles uses lasers

fibre-optic-communication.key_figures: Charles Kao (Nobel Prize 2009); George Hockham; Robert Maurer, Donald Keck, Peter Schultz (Corning)
fibre-optic-communication.summary: Fibre-optic cables carry information as pulses of laser light through ultra-pure glass threads thinner than a human hair.

spaceflight.key_figures: Sergei Korolev (Soviet); Wernher von Braun (US); NASA; Yuri Gagarin; Neil Armstrong
spaceflight.summary: Spaceflight provided the platforms for weather satellites, communications satellites, GPS, Earth observation, and scientific instruments such as the Hubble and James Webb space telescopes.
key_facts: Gagarin: first human in space: 12 April 1961

smartphones.summary: Smartphones are the most rapidly adopted technology in history.

social-media-platforms.key_figures: Mark Zuckerberg (Facebook); Jack Dorsey (Twitter); Kevin Systrom (Instagram); multiple others
social-media-platforms.summary: Social media platforms are the dominant communication infrastructure for personal relationships, journalism, political discourse, marketing, and entertainment in the digital age.

artificial-intelligence.key_figures: Alan Turing; John McCarthy; Geoffrey Hinton; Yann LeCun; Yoshua Bengio
artificial-intelligence.summary: Deep learning's breakthrough (AlexNet, 2012) triggered the modern AI era. AlphaFold (2020) solved the 50-year protein structure problem; LLMs now write, code, and reason.

renewable-energy-technologies.key_figures: Bell Labs (silicon solar cell, 1954); Darrieus and other wind turbine pioneers; multiple researchers
renewable-energy-technologies.summary: Renewable energy technologies — solar photovoltaics, wind turbines, hydropower, geothermal, and tidal — generate electricity without burning fuel and without greenhouse gas emissions.
key_facts: First practical silicon solar cell: Bell Labs, 1954
Solar and wind: ~13% of global electricity (2023)
IEA projects majority of electricity by 2030s

battery-technology.key_figures: Alessandro Volta (electrochemical battery, 1800); John Goodenough, M. Stanley Whittingham, Akira Yoshino (lithium-ion battery — Nobel Prize 2019)
battery-technology.summary: Batteries store electrical energy chemically and release it on demand.

electric-vehicles.key_figures: Charles Jeantaud (France, 1881); GM EV1 (1996); Elon Musk / Martin Eberhard (Tesla, 2003)
electric-vehicles.summary: Electric vehicles eliminate tailpipe emissions, offer lower running costs and simpler mechanics than ICE vehicles, and can be powered by renewable electricity.

robotics.key_figures: George Devol (Unimate, 1961); Joseph Engelberger; Boston Dynamics; Rodney Brooks; Masahiro Mori
robotics.summary: Robotics combines mechanical engineering, sensors, computing, and artificial intelligence to create machines that can perform physical tasks autonomously or semi-autonomously.
key_facts: Industrial robots spread in auto industry: 1970s–90s

3d-printing-additive-manufacturing.key_figures: Chuck Hull (stereolithography, 1984); Carl Deckard (SLS); Scott Crump (FDM, Stratasys)
3d-printing-additive-manufacturing.summary: 3D printing can produce complex three-dimensional objects from digital designs without moulds or subtractive machining.
key_facts: Used for titanium implants, jet engine parts

blockchain.key_figures: Satoshi Nakamoto (pseudonym, identity unknown); Vitalik Buterin (Ethereum)
blockchain.summary: Blockchain technology enables trustless, transparent, immutable record-keeping without a central authority.

cloud-computing.key_figures: Amazon Web Services (Jeff Bezos, Andy Jassy); Salesforce (Marc Benioff, early SaaS); Google; Microsoft
cloud-computing.summary: Cloud computing allows organisations to access computing power, storage, and software without owning or maintaining physical infrastructure.
key_facts: Enables AI, analytics without owning data centres
